questions.explanation: Embedded systems are purpose-built computers integrated into products (appliances, vehicles, sensors) to perform specific tasks, commonly under timing and resource constraints.

options.A: A general-purpose computer designed to run many unrelated applications
options.B: A dedicated computing system built into a larger device to perform specific functions, often with real-time constraints
options.C: A cloud service that provides remote compute resources
options.D: A desktop operating system used for software development

questions.question: What best describes an embedded system?

questions.explanation: Digital logic encodes bits as voltage ranges; logic '1' is typically represented by a voltage near VDD (within specified thresholds).

options.B: A high voltage level near the supply voltage (e.g., 3.3V or 5V), depending on the logic family
options.C: An analog voltage that can take any value

questions.question: In digital systems, what does a logic level '1' typically represent?

questions.explanation: A debugger enables breakpoints, single-stepping, and inspection of memory/registers—core needs for embedded firmware bring-up.
questions.question: Which IDE feature most directly helps you step through firmware line-by-line while inspecting variables and registers on a microcontroller?

questions.explanation: Git is a widely used distributed version control system for tracking source code changes and collaborating across teams.
questions.question: Which tool is most commonly used for software configuration management (version control) in embedded projects?

questions.explanation: Cortex-M commonly uses MSP for exceptions/handler mode and PSP for thread mode (e.g., RTOS tasks), improving isolation and context switching.

options.A: MSP: process stack; PSP: main stack
options.B: MSP: main/exception stack; PSP: thread (task) stack
options.C: MSP: heap pointer; PSP: stack pointer
options.D: MSP: interrupt vector base; PSP: program counter

questions.question: On an Arm Cortex-M microcontroller, which pair correctly matches the typical roles of MSP and PSP?

questions.explanation: Hardware timers provide stable periodic events; an interrupt (or timer-triggered flag) reduces jitter compared to busy-wait loops and polling.

options.A: Use a busy-wait delay loop calibrated by compiler optimization level
options.B: Use a hardware timer to generate an interrupt every 1 ms and trigger sampling in the ISR (or set a flag)
options.C: Poll a GPIO pin in the main loop until it toggles
options.D: Insert a 1 ms sleep call provided by the host OS

questions.question: You need periodic sampling every 1 ms with minimal CPU jitter on a bare-metal Cortex-M. Which approach is most appropriate?

questions.explanation: I2C uses SCL and SDA and includes 7/10-bit addressing, allowing multiple devices to share the same bus.

options.A: UART is synchronous and requires a shared clock line
options.B: I2C typically uses two lines (SCL, SDA) and supports addressing multiple devices on the same bus
options.C: SPI uses device addressing on the data line instead of chip select signals
options.D: I2C is full-duplex by default like SPI

questions.question: Which statement about UART, I2C, and SPI is correct?

questions.explanation: FreeRTOS provides built-in stack overflow hooks and high-water mark measurements to detect insufficient stack and identify which task is at risk.

options.A: Disable interrupts globally and see if the fault disappears
options.B: Enable FreeRTOS stack overflow checking (configCHECK_FOR_STACK_OVERFLOW) and use high-water mark APIs (uxTaskGetStackHighWaterMark) to monitor stack usage
options.C: Increase CPU clock frequency to reduce task runtime
options.D: Replace all queues with global variables to reduce overhead

questions.question: A FreeRTOS-based product occasionally hard-faults after running for hours. You suspect a stack overflow in one task. Which combination is the best first step to detect and diagnose it?

questions.explanation: Wait queues allow a process to sleep efficiently until an event occurs; the driver wakes it when data is available, avoiding CPU waste.

options.A: Use udelay() in the read() implementation until data is ready
options.B: Use a wait queue (wait_event_interruptible) and wake_up() from the interrupt handler or bottom half when data arrives
options.C: Call printf() from kernel space to notify user space
options.D: Use malloc() in the driver to allocate more memory for faster reads

questions.question: On Embedded Linux, you are writing a character device driver for a sensor. User space should read samples via read(). Which kernel mechanism is most appropriate to block until new data arrives without busy-waiting?

questions.explanation: Model-based code generation is a common automatic programming approach in embedded systems, while Prolog/CLP is suited to expressing and solving rule/constraint problems like configuration validity.

options.A: Automatic programming: model-based code generation (e.g., Simulink); Logic programming: Prolog/constraint logic programming to encode rules and constraints
options.B: Automatic programming: manual copy-paste templates; Logic programming: C preprocessor macros
options.C: Automatic programming: writing assembly by hand; Logic programming: using only unit tests
options.D: Automatic programming: using a text editor; Logic programming: using Git branches

questions.question: You want to auto-generate embedded C code from a high-level specification and also use logic programming to express constraints (e.g., valid pin mappings). Which pairing best matches these goals?
